title: Yayla çorbası
subtitle: Turkish yoghurt and rice soup with dried mint butter
excerpt: A soup of yoghurt and rice, stabilised with egg and flour so the yoghurt does not curdle, finished with dried mint butter poured over sizzling. Silky, slightly sour, deeply comforting. It takes fifteen minutes once the rice is cooked.
sourceNotes: Yayla çorbası (the name means highland or plateau soup, referring to the yoghurt-producing pastoral uplands of Anatolia) is documented in Turkish household and regional cooking literature from the nineteenth century. Yoghurt-based soups are common throughout central and eastern Anatolia and into the Caucasus, reflecting the pastoral culture of the region. The egg-and-flour stabilisation technique to prevent yoghurt from splitting in hot liquid is documented in Ottoman and Turkish cooking literature.

content.text: The key technical step in this soup is tempering the yoghurt. Full-fat yoghurt heated too quickly in an acid environment curdles into unpleasant lumps. Adding beaten egg and flour to the yoghurt first raises its curdling point significantly, and adding the hot stock to the yoghurt mixture gradually rather than the other way round means it never encounters sudden heat. Take these steps seriously and the soup will be silky; skip either and it will not.

The dried mint butter is not optional. Dried mint in hot butter becomes aromatic in seconds and transforms an unremarkable-looking soup into something with a distinct character. The butter goes on at the table, poured sizzling over each bowl.

items.prepNote: full-fat; do not use low-fat or Greek yoghurt for this

content.text: Bring the {{stock-vegetable}} of stock to a boil in a large saucepan. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of rice and cook for 15 minutes until tender. Reduce the heat to very low.

In a large bowl, whisk the {{eggs}} together with the {{plain-flour}} of flour until smooth. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}} of yoghurt and whisk until completely combined. Now add the hot stock from the pan to the yoghurt mixture a ladle at a time, whisking constantly, until you have added about a third of the stock. This tempers the yoghurt so it does not curdle. Pour the tempered mixture back into the saucepan with the remaining stock and rice. Stir constantly over medium-low heat for 3 to 4 minutes until the soup is hot and thickened slightly but not boiling. Season with salt.

Ladle the soup into warm bowls. In a small pan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat until foaming. Add the {{mint-dried}} of dried mint and {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika. Swirl for 15 seconds. Pour the sizzling mint butter over each bowl immediately. Serve at once.

items.cause: Stock added too fast; soup brought to a boil; low-fat yoghurt used
items.fix: Add the stock a ladle at a time while whisking; never let the soup boil; use full-fat yoghurt only

items.cause: Not enough flour in the yoghurt mixture, or rice too little
items.fix: The soup thickens slightly as it heats; if still thin, mix 1 tbsp flour with a little cold water and whisk in, then heat gently

items.cause: Yoghurt very acidic; mint butter not added
items.fix: Use a mild full-fat yoghurt; the butter and mint soften the sourness — do not skip the finishing step

items.cause: Mint added before the butter was hot; or dried mint was old
items.fix: Add the mint to already-foaming butter and pour immediately; use dried mint within 12 months of opening

content.text: Yayla çorbası belongs to the pastoral interior of Anatolia, where yoghurt has been produced from sheep and goat milk for millennia and forms the basis of a significant portion of the regional diet. The highland villages (yayla) that give the soup its name are summer pastures at altitude, where herdsmen would move with their animals to find grazing. The food of these upland communities was built around what could be produced and stored without refrigeration: dried grains, cured meats, and fermented dairy. Yoghurt soup is its most direct expression. Outside Turkey, it is served in Turkish restaurants throughout Europe and appears on tables at home as a reliable, restorative dish for cold weather.
